questions.question: What is attention fragmentation?

options.text: The total number of hours spent on screens per day
The frequent switching between tasks and contexts, driven by interruptions and notifications, which impairs deep focus
A clinical attention disorder requiring medication

questions.explanation: Attention fragmentation refers to the splitting of attention across multiple tasks and contexts due to interruptions (notifications, messages, alerts), which reduces the ability to sustain deep, focused attention.

questions.question: What does research show about task-switching and productivity?

options.text: Multitasking increases productivity by allowing you to do more simultaneously
Task-switching imposes cognitive costs (switching time, reduced accuracy) and can reduce productivity by 40%
Only people with ADHD experience task-switching costs
Task-switching has no impact on work quality

questions.explanation: Research shows task-switching imposes significant cognitive costs: time to switch contexts, reduced accuracy, and up to 40% productivity loss. What feels like multitasking is actually rapid switching with costs.

questions.question: Which of the following is a consequence of chronic attention fragmentation?

options.text: Improved ability to focus deeply over time
Reduced capacity for sustained attention, increased mental fatigue, and impaired learning

questions.explanation: Chronic fragmentation reduces the capacity for sustained attention, increases mental fatigue, impairs learning and memory consolidation, and can reduce overall wellbeing.

questions.question: What is the relationship between notifications and attention fragmentation?

options.text: Notifications have no impact on attention — you can easily ignore them
Even notifications you don't respond to create attention residue and cognitive load, fragmenting focus
Notifications improve focus by keeping you engaged

questions.explanation: Research shows that even unattended notifications create 'attention residue' — part of your mind remains thinking about the notification, fragmenting focus. The mere presence of notifications reduces cognitive performance.

questions.question: Estimate how many times your attention is fragmented by notifications or self-interruptions (checking phone, email, messages) during a typical work or study hour. How does this impact your ability to think deeply?
